title: Union Budget 2026: FM Targets 6.8% GDP Growth, Fiscal Deficit Pegged at 4.4% of GDP
content:
Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman on Sunday presented the Union Budget 2026-27, projecting a nominal GDP growth rate of 10.1% and a real GDP growth target of 6.8% for the upcoming fiscal year. The fiscal deficit has been pegged at 4.4% of GDP, down from the revised estimate of 4.8% for FY26, signalling the government's continued commitment to fiscal consolidation.

The Budget estimates total expenditure at Rs 52.8 lakh crore, up 8.2% from the revised estimates of FY26. Revenue receipts are projected at Rs 35.6 lakh crore, buoyed by expectations of robust direct tax collections following the overhaul of personal income tax slabs.

"We are firmly on the glide path to bring the fiscal deficit below 4% by FY28," Sitharaman said in her Budget speech, adding that the government's borrowing programme would be calibrated to ensure adequate liquidity for private sector credit growth.

Capital expenditure has been raised to Rs 12.5 lakh crore, a 14.7% jump over the previous year, with a significant allocation towards railways (Rs 2.8 lakh crore), highways (Rs 2.1 lakh crore), and urban infrastructure (Rs 1.4 lakh crore). The effective capital expenditure, including grants to states for capex, stands at Rs 15.2 lakh crore.

Chief Economic Adviser V Anantha Nageswaran noted that the growth projection was conservative and achievable. "The Indian economy has demonstrated remarkable resilience. With the structural reforms embedded in this Budget, we expect growth momentum to accelerate in the second half of FY27," he said at the post-Budget press conference.

The Budget also introduces a new Medium-Term Fiscal Framework that commits to reducing the debt-to-GDP ratio to 52% by FY30 from the current 57.1%. This includes a automatic stabilizer mechanism that would trigger expenditure adjustments if revenue collections fall short by more than 5% of budget estimates.

On the revenue side, the government expects GST collections to grow at 12.5% in FY27, supported by continued improvements in compliance and the expansion of the tax base. Disinvestment targets have been set at Rs 65,000 crore, with strategic sales of IDBI Bank and Concor expected to contribute the bulk.

Economists have broadly welcomed the fiscal math. "The numbers are credible and the fiscal consolidation path is intact. The emphasis on capex while maintaining fiscal discipline is commendable," said Pranjul Bhandari, chief India economist at HSBC.
author: ET Bureau

title: Government's Rs 16.8 Lakh Crore Borrowing Plan Keeps Bond Market on Edge
content:
The Union Budget 2026-27 has pegged the gross market borrowing programme at Rs 16.8 lakh crore, marginally higher than the Street's expectation of Rs 16.2-16.5 lakh crore. Net borrowing, after accounting for redemptions of Rs 4.1 lakh crore, is estimated at Rs 12.7 lakh crore.

The borrowing programme will be front-loaded, with 55% of gross issuance planned for the first half of FY27. The government has indicated it will diversify its borrowing instruments, with plans to issue sovereign green bonds worth Rs 45,000 crore and inflation-indexed bonds of Rs 20,000 crore during the year.

Bond yields reacted with a brief spike following the announcement. The benchmark 10-year government security yield rose 6 basis points to 6.82% immediately after the Budget speech, before settling at 6.78% by the close of trading. Traders said the borrowing number was within the range of expectations, though on the higher side.

"The gross borrowing is about Rs 30,000-40,000 crore higher than the most optimistic estimates, but the net borrowing is manageable given the RBI's likely support through open market operations," said Vijay Sharma, head of fixed income at ICICI Prudential AMC. "We expect the RBI to conduct OMO purchases of Rs 2-3 lakh crore during FY27 to manage liquidity."

The Budget also announced the introduction of a new 50-year government bond, aimed at insurance companies and pension funds seeking ultra-long duration assets. This is expected to help extend the yield curve and provide a benchmark for long-term infrastructure financing.

A positive surprise was the government's plan to reduce the share of short-term borrowings (treasury bills) in total borrowing from 18% to 14%, which analysts said would reduce rollover risks. The weighted average maturity of new issuances is targeted at 16.2 years, up from 15.8 years in FY26.

The fiscal deficit financing mix also includes Rs 80,000 crore from the National Small Savings Fund and Rs 35,000 crore from external borrowings, including a planned $3 billion sovereign bond issuance in international markets.

"The borrowing plan is well-structured. The introduction of the 50-year bond and the green bond expansion shows the government is serious about deepening the bond market," said Suyash Choudhary, head of fixed income at Bandhan AMC.
author: Sarita Khanna

title: Budget 2026: Inflation Target Retained at 4%, FM Banks on Food Price Moderation
content:
The Union Budget 2026-27 retains the inflation target at 4% with a tolerance band of +/- 2%, while announcing a series of supply-side measures aimed at keeping food prices in check. The Budget assumes average CPI inflation of 4.3% for FY27, down from an estimated 4.7% in the current fiscal.

Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman announced the creation of a Rs 15,000 crore Price Stabilization Fund specifically for vegetables and pulses, building on the existing buffer stock mechanism. The fund will be used to intervene in markets during price spikes, particularly for tomatoes, onions, and potatoes — the so-called TOP commodities that have caused inflation volatility in recent years.

"Food inflation has been a persistent challenge. This Budget takes a multi-pronged approach — from cold chain infrastructure to direct market intervention — to ensure price stability," Sitharaman said.

The Budget allocates Rs 8,500 crore for cold chain and warehouse infrastructure, a 40% increase from FY26. This includes the establishment of 200 new integrated cold chain facilities and the upgrade of 500 existing ones across major agricultural production hubs. The government expects this investment to reduce post-harvest losses from the current 12-15% to below 8% within three years.

Customs duty on edible oils has been reduced from 12.5% to 7.5% for crude palm oil and from 15% to 10% for refined oils, a measure expected to provide immediate relief to cooking oil prices. The duty reduction on pulses imports has been extended by another year.

The Budget also announces a new Digital Agriculture Mission with an allocation of Rs 6,000 crore, aimed at improving crop forecasting and enabling better price discovery through a unified national agricultural market platform.

Former RBI Deputy Governor Viral Acharya commented that the supply-side measures were welcome but insufficient. "The structural drivers of food inflation — fragmented supply chains, inadequate storage, and climate volatility — require sustained multi-year investment. The Budget makes a start, but we need to see follow-through," he told ET.

The government has also committed to maintaining buffer stocks of key commodities at levels 20% above the five-year average, providing an additional cushion against price shocks. Import duties on 15 essential food items have been rationalized to ensure timely availability during domestic shortfalls.
author: Deepshikha Sikarwar

title: Budget 2026: Centre's Capex Push to Sustain Growth Engine, GDP Multiplier Effect Expected at 2.5x
content:
The Union Budget 2026-27 has doubled down on the capital expenditure strategy that has been the hallmark of the Modi government's economic policy, raising the capex outlay to Rs 12.5 lakh crore — a 14.7% increase over FY26 revised estimates and nearly four times the Rs 3.36 lakh crore allocated in FY21.

The government's own estimates suggest a GDP multiplier effect of 2.5x on public capex, implying that every rupee spent on capital expenditure generates Rs 2.50 of economic output over a three-year period. This is significantly higher than the 0.9x multiplier estimated for revenue expenditure.

The sectoral allocation reveals a clear focus on transport infrastructure and energy transition. Railways receive Rs 2.8 lakh crore (up 18% YoY), highways and roads get Rs 2.1 lakh crore (up 12%), and a new green energy transition fund has been created with Rs 75,000 crore for solar, wind, and green hydrogen projects.

Urban infrastructure receives a major boost with Rs 1.4 lakh crore allocated for metro projects, water supply, and smart city initiatives. Notably, 12 new metro corridors have been sanctioned across tier-2 cities including Lucknow, Jaipur, Bhopal, and Coimbatore.

"The capex allocation is in line with our expectations and reinforces the investment-led growth strategy," said Neelkanth Mishra, chief economist at Axis Bank. "The key positive is the emphasis on quality of spending — more on asset creation and less on maintenance. This should improve the asset utilization ratio of public infrastructure."

The Budget introduces a new performance-linked incentive for states that exceed their capex targets. States that spend more than 90% of their capex allocation will receive a bonus grant equivalent to 10% of the excess spending. This is expected to address the persistent problem of underspending by state governments, which utilized only 72% of their capex allocation in FY26.

The private sector capex cycle, which has been showing signs of revival, is expected to receive a further boost from the Budget's production-linked incentive (PLI) schemes. The Budget extends PLI to three new sectors — toys, furniture, and technical textiles — with a total outlay of Rs 18,000 crore over five years.

Defence capital expenditure has been raised to Rs 2.1 lakh crore, with 75% earmarked for domestic procurement. The emphasis on indigenization is expected to benefit domestic defence manufacturers and boost the 'Make in India' initiative.

"India's capex-to-GDP ratio is now at 4.3%, one of the highest among major economies. This sustained investment cycle is creating a virtuous loop of infrastructure creation, job generation, and productivity improvement," said Ridham Desai, managing director at Morgan Stanley India.
author: ET Bureau

title: Budget 2026 Winners: IT, AI Startups Get Rs 25,000 Crore Boost; STPI Tax Breaks Extended
content:
The Union Budget 2026-27 has delivered a substantial fillip to the technology sector, with a cumulative outlay of Rs 25,000 crore for IT infrastructure, artificial intelligence, and startup ecosystem development. The budget also extends the Software Technology Parks of India (STPI) tax benefits by three years to March 2029 and introduces a new 'Digital India Innovation Fund' with a corpus of Rs 10,000 crore.

The centrepiece of the tech allocation is the National AI Mission, which receives Rs 8,500 crore — a threefold increase from FY26. This includes Rs 3,000 crore for building sovereign AI compute infrastructure, Rs 2,500 crore for AI research and development, and Rs 3,000 crore for AI applications in governance, healthcare, and agriculture.

"India has a unique opportunity to become the AI talent capital of the world. This Budget provides the foundational investment to realize that ambition," said Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman during her speech.

For startups, the Budget extends the tax holiday under Section 80-IAC by two years to March 2028 and raises the turnover eligibility threshold from Rs 100 crore to Rs 150 crore. Angel tax, which was partially rolled back in 2024, has been fully abolished for all DPIIT-registered startups, removing a longstanding irritant for the ecosystem.

The IT services industry, which employs over 5.4 million people, benefits from the extension of the safe harbour rules for transfer pricing and a new 'Global Capability Centre (GCC) Acceleration Scheme' that offers a 15% investment allowance on capital expenditure for setting up or expanding GCCs in tier-2 and tier-3 cities.

"The GCC incentive is a game-changer. We were already seeing interest from multinationals to move beyond Bengaluru and Hyderabad. This will accelerate the trend significantly," said Debjani Ghosh, president of NASSCOM.

Shares of major IT companies rallied post-Budget. Infosys rose 3.2%, TCS gained 2.1%, and Wipro was up 2.8%. Mid-cap IT stocks saw even sharper gains, with Persistent Systems up 5.4% and Coforge rising 4.7%, as investors bet on increased government digitization spending.

The Budget also allocates Rs 4,500 crore for cybersecurity infrastructure, including the establishment of a National Cyber Coordination Centre and a Cyber Security Skills Academy that aims to train 500,000 professionals over three years.

Semiconductor manufacturing gets continued support with Rs 6,000 crore allocated for the India Semiconductor Mission, on top of existing commitments. The customs duty exemption on semiconductor manufacturing equipment has been extended for five years.
author: Raghu Krishnan

title: Pharma and Healthcare Stocks Rally as Budget 2026 Ups Health Spending to 2.5% of GDP
content:
The pharmaceutical and healthcare sectors emerged as clear winners in the Union Budget 2026-27, with the total health expenditure rising to 2.5% of GDP — up from 2.1% in FY26 and a significant step towards the National Health Policy target of 2.5% by 2025 (now achieved a year late).

The total allocation for the Ministry of Health stands at Rs 1.12 lakh crore, a 22% increase from the previous year. Within this, the Ayushman Bharat scheme receives Rs 12,000 crore, with coverage expanded to include all senior citizens above 70 years regardless of income, adding an estimated 4.5 crore new beneficiaries.

For the pharmaceutical industry, the Budget introduces a 'Pharma Innovation Fund' of Rs 5,000 crore to support drug discovery and biosimilar development by Indian companies. The weighted tax deduction on R&D expenditure has been enhanced from 100% to 150% for pharmaceutical companies investing in novel drug research.

"This is the most pharma-friendly budget in recent memory. The R&D incentive alone could unlock Rs 15,000-20,000 crore of private sector investment in drug discovery over the next five years," said Dilip Shanghvi, managing director of Sun Pharmaceuticals.

The PLI scheme for pharmaceuticals has been extended with an additional Rs 8,000 crore allocation focused on active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) where India currently depends on China for 65-70% of its requirements. The target is to reduce import dependence to below 40% by 2030.

Medical devices manufacturing receives a customs duty restructuring, with import duties on critical raw materials reduced to zero while duties on finished medical devices are raised from 10% to 15%. This is expected to incentivize domestic manufacturing and support the 'Make in India' initiative in healthcare.

The Nifty Pharma index surged 3.8% on Budget day, with Cipla (up 5.1%), Dr Reddy's (up 4.3%), and Divi's Labs (up 6.2%) leading the gains. Hospital stocks also rallied, with Apollo Hospitals gaining 4.5% and Max Healthcare up 3.9%, driven by the Ayushman Bharat expansion.

The Budget also allocates Rs 7,500 crore for setting up 150 new medical colleges and upgrading 200 existing district hospitals. Digital health infrastructure receives Rs 3,000 crore for the expansion of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission, targeting 100% digitization of public health records by 2028.

"The combination of demand-side measures like Ayushman Bharat expansion and supply-side investments in pharma manufacturing and R&D creates a comprehensive growth framework for the healthcare sector," said Aditya Khemka, fund manager at InCred Healthcare Fund.
author: Vikas Srivastava

title: Banking Sector Gets Reform Push: Budget 2026 Announces Deposit Insurance Hike, Bad Bank Expansion
content:
The banking and financial services sector received a significant reform push in the Union Budget 2026-27, with Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman announcing an increase in deposit insurance cover from Rs 5 lakh to Rs 10 lakh, a move that had been long demanded by depositors and banking experts.

The enhanced deposit insurance, to be implemented by October 2026, will protect an estimated 98.5% of all deposit accounts in terms of number (up from the current 97.8%). The Deposit Insurance and Credit Guarantee Corporation (DICGC) premium, currently at 15 paise per Rs 100 of deposits, will be raised to 20 paise to fund the expanded coverage.

"The deposit insurance hike is a confidence-building measure that will help attract more savings into the formal banking system, particularly from rural and semi-urban areas," said Rajnish Kumar, former chairman of State Bank of India.

The Budget also announced an expansion of the National Asset Reconstruction Company (NARCL), the so-called 'bad bank', with an additional government guarantee of Rs 50,000 crore. NARCL has so far resolved or acquired Rs 78,000 crore of non-performing assets since its inception and the additional support is expected to help it absorb another Rs 1.2 lakh crore of stressed assets from bank balance sheets.

Public sector bank recapitalization receives Rs 25,000 crore, with the government emphasizing that this is the last tranche of capital support as PSBs are now expected to be self-sustaining through internal accruals. The government's stake sale roadmap for PSBs has been updated, with plans to reduce its holding in Bank of Maharashtra and Central Bank of India to below 51% through offer-for-sale mechanisms.

The Budget introduces a new regulatory framework for digital lending platforms, including mandatory registration with the RBI and a cap on interest rates for microloans at 24% per annum. This follows concerns about predatory lending practices in the fintech space.

Bank stocks showed a mixed reaction. While the Nifty Bank index gained 1.8%, individual stock movements varied. SBI rose 2.4%, HDFC Bank gained 1.9%, but ICICI Bank dipped 0.3% on profit booking. PSU bank stocks rallied sharply, with Bank of Baroda up 4.2% and Punjab National Bank gaining 3.8%.

The priority sector lending norms have been revised to include climate finance as a separate sub-category with a 5% target. Banks will be required to direct at least 5% of their adjusted net bank credit towards green and climate adaptation projects.

"The banking reforms in this Budget are pragmatic and forward-looking. The digital lending framework was overdue, and the deposit insurance hike will strengthen financial inclusion," said Uday Kotak, founder of Kotak Mahindra Bank.
author: Saloni Shukla

title: Real Estate Gets Budget Bonanza: Higher HRA Exemption, SWAMIH Fund 2.0, Stamp Duty Incentives
content:
The real estate sector received a multi-layered boost in the Union Budget 2026-27, with measures spanning tax incentives for homebuyers, affordable housing stimulus, and completion of stalled projects. The package is expected to drive a 15-20% increase in housing demand in metropolitan cities, according to industry estimates.

The most impactful measure for homebuyers is the increase in the House Rent Allowance (HRA) exemption limit. For metro cities (Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata, Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Pune, and Ahmedabad — with the last two being newly added), the exemption limit has been raised from 50% to 60% of basic salary. For non-metro cities, it goes up from 40% to 50%. This is expected to benefit over 2 crore salaried employees.

The interest deduction on home loans under Section 24(b) has been enhanced from Rs 2 lakh to Rs 3 lakh for self-occupied properties, a move that effectively reduces the EMI burden for new homebuyers by Rs 25,000-30,000 per year for those in the 30% tax bracket.

For affordable housing, the government launches SWAMIH Fund 2.0 with a corpus of Rs 25,000 crore, building on the success of the original fund that has completed over 46,000 housing units across 130 projects since 2019. The new fund will prioritize projects in the Rs 30-75 lakh price range in metro cities.

The Budget also introduces a 'Stamp Duty Incentive Scheme' where the central government will reimburse 50% of stamp duty paid on first-time home purchases up to Rs 75 lakh, subject to states agreeing to cap stamp duty at 5%. Six states — Maharashtra, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Uttar Pradesh, Rajasthan, and Madhya Pradesh — have already expressed interest in participating.

"This is the most comprehensive housing stimulus package we have seen in any budget. The combination of HRA hike, higher interest deduction, and stamp duty incentive creates a powerful demand impulse," said Niranjan Hiranandani, chairman of NAREDCO.

Real estate stocks soared on the announcements. DLF jumped 7.2%, Godrej Properties gained 6.8%, Oberoi Realty was up 5.5%, and Prestige Estates rose 6.1%. Cement stocks also rallied in sympathy, with UltraTech Cement up 3.4% and Ambuja Cements gaining 2.9%.

The PMAY-Urban scheme receives Rs 35,000 crore, with the target of completing 1.2 crore affordable housing units by 2027. The credit-linked subsidy component has been revived with a new income ceiling of Rs 12 lakh per annum.

"The real estate sector is a massive employment generator with backward and forward linkages to over 270 allied industries. This Budget recognizes its strategic importance to the economy," said Pirojsha Godrej, executive chairman of Godrej Properties.
author: Kailash Babar

title: Sensex Surges 890 Points on Budget Day, Nifty Closes Above 24,800; Broad-Based Rally
content:
Indian equity markets delivered a resounding verdict on the Union Budget 2026-27, with the BSE Sensex surging 890 points (1.12%) to close at 80,345 and the Nifty 50 gaining 267 points (1.09%) to end at 24,823. The rally was broad-based, with 2,387 stocks advancing against 612 declining on the BSE.

The market's euphoria was driven by a combination of factors: the larger-than-expected personal income tax cuts, the sustained capex push, and sector-specific incentives for IT, pharma, and real estate. The Nifty Midcap 100 index outperformed, rising 2.3%, while the Nifty Smallcap 100 gained 2.7%, reflecting optimism about domestic demand-driven sectors.

Sectoral indices painted a picture of widespread optimism. The Nifty Realty index was the top performer, surging 5.8%, followed by Nifty Pharma (3.8%), Nifty IT (2.9%), and Nifty Auto (2.4%). The only sectoral index in the red was Nifty FMCG, which dipped 0.4% on concerns about higher excise duties on cigarettes and sugar-sweetened beverages.

"The Budget has something for everyone — tax relief for the middle class, capex push for infrastructure, sectoral incentives for IT and pharma, and housing stimulus for real estate. It's a rare budget that manages to balance populism with prudence," said Raamdeo Agrawal, chairman of Motilal Oswal Financial Services.

The market breadth was strongly positive throughout the session, though there was a brief wobble around 12:30 PM when the borrowing numbers came in slightly higher than expected. Bond yields spiked briefly but the equity market shrugged it off as the fiscal deficit number of 4.4% was in line with expectations.

Options data showed heavy call writing at the 25,000 strike, suggesting that the market may face resistance at that level in the near term. However, put writing at 24,500 indicates strong support. The India VIX dropped 8.2% to 13.4, suggesting reduced uncertainty post-Budget.

Among individual stocks, DLF (+7.2%), Cipla (+5.1%), and Persistent Systems (+5.4%) were the top gainers. ITC was the biggest loser, dropping 2.8% on the cigarette excise hike.

Total market turnover on the NSE crossed Rs 1.2 lakh crore, the highest for a Budget day session since 2021. Delivery-based volumes accounted for 42% of total turnover, higher than the usual 32-35%, indicating genuine buying interest rather than speculative trading.

"The market is pricing in the growth impulse from the Budget measures. We maintain our Nifty target of 27,500 for December 2026, and see this Budget as a significant positive catalyst," said Gautam Duggad, head of research at Motilal Oswal Institutional Equities.
author: Rajesh Mascarenhas

title: FII Flows Turn Positive on Budget Day After 4-Month Drought; Rs 4,200 Crore Net Buying
content:
Foreign institutional investors (FIIs) turned net buyers in Indian equities on Budget day, pumping in Rs 4,200 crore — their largest single-day inflow since October 2025. This comes after a prolonged period of net selling that saw FIIs withdraw Rs 1.18 lakh crore from Indian markets between October 2025 and January 2026.

The FII buying was concentrated in financial services (Rs 1,800 crore), IT (Rs 950 crore), and healthcare (Rs 680 crore), according to provisional exchange data. Domestic institutional investors (DIIs) were also net buyers at Rs 2,100 crore, though this was below their recent daily average of Rs 3,500 crore, suggesting that foreign buying reduced the need for domestic support.

"The Budget has addressed several concerns that foreign investors had about India — fiscal discipline, tax policy predictability, and infrastructure investment. The full abolition of angel tax and the GCC incentives are particularly positive signals for global capital," said Mark Matthews, head of research at Julius Baer Asia.

The rupee strengthened to 85.62 against the US dollar from its pre-Budget level of 85.95, reflecting improved foreign investor sentiment. Currency traders said the fiscal deficit number of 4.4% and the commitment to a glide path below 4% were key reassurance factors.

However, market participants cautioned that one day of positive flows does not constitute a trend reversal. FII allocation to India has been declining as a share of emerging market portfolios, from 18% in early 2024 to 14.5% currently, with funds being redirected to China following its recent stimulus measures.

"India's valuations remain a concern at 21x forward earnings versus the EM average of 12x. But the Budget's growth-supportive measures could justify a premium over time. We are upgrading India from underweight to neutral in our EM portfolio," said Jonathan Garner, chief Asia and emerging markets strategist at Morgan Stanley.

The MSCI India index rose 1.3% on Budget day, outperforming the MSCI Emerging Markets index which was flat. India's weight in the MSCI EM index currently stands at 18.2%, the second highest after China at 27.4%.

ETF flows also showed encouraging signs. The iShares MSCI India ETF (INDA) saw inflows of $180 million on Friday (US trading), the highest daily inflow since July 2025. The WisdomTree India Earnings Fund (EPI) also attracted $45 million.

"The Budget is a reminder that India's structural growth story remains intact. Global investors who have been underweight India may use this as an entry point. We expect FII flows to gradually turn positive in Q1 FY27," said Ridham Desai, managing director at Morgan Stanley India.
author: Bhaskar Dutta

title: Bond Yields Settle at 6.78% After Budget; Traders See RBI Rate Cut Window Opening
content:
Government bond yields settled at 6.78% after an eventful Budget day trading session that saw the 10-year benchmark oscillate between 6.72% and 6.85%. The yield ended 4 basis points higher than the previous close, a relatively muted reaction given the slightly above-expectation borrowing programme of Rs 16.8 lakh crore.

The bond market's initial reaction was negative, with yields spiking to 6.85% immediately after the borrowing numbers were announced. However, selling pressure eased as traders digested the full picture — the fiscal deficit of 4.4% was in line with expectations, and the commitment to further consolidation provided a medium-term positive anchor.

"The market was bracing for worse. The fiscal deficit number is credible and the glide path is intact. We think bond yields will trend lower over the next 6-9 months, especially if the RBI starts cutting rates," said Mahendra Jajoo, CIO - fixed income at Mirae Asset Investment Managers.

The Budget's fiscal math has strengthened the case for RBI rate cuts, according to multiple bond market participants. With the government showing discipline on the expenditure side and inflation projected at 4.3%, the RBI's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) has more room to reduce rates. The consensus now expects a 25 basis point cut at the April 2026 policy meeting, with a total of 50-75 basis points of cuts during FY27.

The yield curve steepened slightly on Budget day, with the spread between 2-year and 10-year government securities widening to 45 basis points from 38 basis points. This reflects expectations that the RBI will ease at the short end while supply pressure keeps long-term yields elevated.

Corporate bond spreads tightened marginally, with AAA-rated 5-year corporate bonds now yielding 7.35%, a spread of 57 basis points over the equivalent government security. Traders expect the spread to compress further as the fiscal consolidation reduces government crowding out of private credit.

The introduction of the 50-year government bond was received with cautious optimism. Insurance companies and pension funds, which have long-duration liabilities, are expected to be natural buyers. The Life Insurance Corporation of India indicated it would participate actively in the new segment.

"The 50-year bond fills a gap in the Indian yield curve. It will help insurance companies and pension funds better match their asset-liability profiles. We expect initial issuance of Rs 20,000-25,000 crore in this segment," said Badrish Kulhalli, head of fixed income at HDFC Life.

The government's plan to issue Rs 45,000 crore of sovereign green bonds is also expected to attract ESG-focused investors, potentially bringing new buyers into the Indian bond market and reducing the overall cost of borrowing.
author: Bhaskar Dutta

title: Former RBI Governor Raghuram Rajan: 'Budget Gets Fiscal Balance Right, But Job Creation Remains the Missing Piece'
content:
Former Reserve Bank of India Governor Raghuram Rajan offered a nuanced assessment of the Union Budget 2026-27, praising its fiscal discipline while flagging concerns about the adequacy of employment generation measures.

In an exclusive interview with ET, Rajan said the fiscal deficit target of 4.4% was "credible and achievable" and that the capex push was the right strategy. "The government has correctly identified that investment-led growth is more sustainable than consumption-led growth. The capex allocation of Rs 12.5 lakh crore, if effectively spent, can crowd in significant private investment," he said.

However, Rajan was critical of what he called the Budget's insufficient focus on job creation. "India needs to create 8-10 million non-farm jobs annually just to absorb new entrants into the workforce. The Budget's employment measures — the intern scheme, the PLI extensions — are incremental. We need a more fundamental rethink of our labor market strategy."

Rajan pointed to the new tax slabs as a step in the right direction for consumption, noting that the Rs 50,000 increase in the standard deduction and the higher exemption limits would put Rs 45,000-50,000 annually back in the pockets of middle-class taxpayers. "This will boost urban consumption, which has been sluggish. But the real consumption weakness is in rural India, and the Budget's rural allocation, while adequate, is not transformative."

On the financial sector reforms, Rajan praised the deposit insurance hike to Rs 10 lakh. "This should have been done years ago. The Rs 5 lakh limit was set in 2020 and hadn't kept pace with inflation. The new limit provides meaningful protection and will bolster confidence in the banking system."

He was more cautious about the NARCL expansion, warning that the bad bank model has inherent limitations. "The challenge is not just transferring NPAs off bank books — it's resolving them and recovering value. NARCL needs to demonstrate faster resolution timelines. The additional Rs 50,000 crore guarantee is welcome, but execution is key."

On the macro framework, Rajan noted that the GDP growth projection of 6.8% was realistic but not ambitious enough. "India should be targeting 7.5-8% growth given its demographic dividend. To achieve that, we need deeper structural reforms — in land, labor, and agriculture — that go beyond what any single budget can deliver."

Regarding the global economic context, Rajan cautioned that India's growth could face headwinds from slowing global trade and potential US tariff escalations. "The Budget wisely focuses on domestic demand drivers. But we shouldn't be complacent about external risks. The government should have a contingency plan for a global slowdown scenario."

Rajan also commented on the green energy transition fund. "The Rs 75,000 crore allocation is significant, but India needs Rs 10-15 lakh crore of investment in clean energy over the next decade. The government should use this fund to catalyze private investment through blended finance structures rather than trying to fund the entire transition from the budget."
author: Bodhisatva Ganguli

title: HDFC Bank Chief Economist: Budget's Consumption Boost Could Add 30-40 bps to GDP Growth
content:
Abheek Barua, chief economist at HDFC Bank, estimates that the Union Budget 2026-27's combined impact of income tax cuts, HRA enhancements, and rural spending could add 30-40 basis points to GDP growth in FY27, potentially pushing the actual growth rate to 7-7.2% against the government's conservative estimate of 6.8%.

In a detailed analysis shared exclusively with ET, Barua said the income tax relief would release approximately Rs 1.1 lakh crore of disposable income into the economy. "The tax cuts are well-targeted at the Rs 7-20 lakh income bracket, which has the highest marginal propensity to consume. We estimate that 65-70% of this additional income will be spent rather than saved, creating a demand multiplier of 1.8-2x."

Barua highlighted four transmission channels through which the Budget would impact growth:

First, the direct consumption effect from tax cuts and higher HRA exemptions. "The HRA change alone benefits 2 crore salaried employees. Combined with the standard deduction increase and tax slab rationalization, the total consumption boost could be Rs 70,000-80,000 crore in FY27."

Second, the capex multiplier. "The Rs 12.5 lakh crore capital expenditure allocation has a GDP multiplier of 2.5x over three years. The immediate first-year impact would be around Rs 4-5 lakh crore of additional economic activity, supporting construction, cement, steel, and capital goods sectors."

Third, the housing market stimulus. "The real estate measures — HRA hike, higher Section 24(b) limit, SWAMIH 2.0, and stamp duty incentives — could drive a 15-20% increase in housing demand. Given the large multiplier effect of construction (every Rs 1 of housing investment generates Rs 2.7 of economic output), this is a significant growth driver."

Fourth, the rural economy boost. "The agriculture and rural allocation of Rs 5.2 lakh crore, including higher MSPs, the Price Stabilization Fund, and the Digital Agriculture Mission, addresses the rural demand weakness that has been a drag on growth for the past two years."

Barua cautioned, however, that the growth upside depends on execution and global conditions. "If the government achieves 85%+ utilization of its capex allocation (versus 78% in FY26) and global growth doesn't deteriorate sharply, we could see 7%+ growth. But execution risks remain significant, particularly at the state level."

On inflation, Barua said the supply-side measures in the Budget were positive but the demand boost from tax cuts could create some upward pressure. "Net-net, we expect CPI inflation to average 4.2-4.5% in FY27, within the RBI's comfort zone. This should allow for 50-75 basis points of rate cuts."

He also noted that the fiscal math was sound but tight. "Revenue assumptions, particularly on direct tax growth of 14.5%, are optimistic but not unreasonable given the base effect and the broadening of the tax net. GST growth of 12.5% is also achievable. The risk is on the disinvestment target of Rs 65,000 crore, which has historically been difficult to achieve."
author: Gayatri Nayak

title: Top Fund Managers React: 'Budget Creates Clear Investing Roadmap for FY27'
content:
Leading fund managers across Indian mutual funds and portfolio management services have largely welcomed the Union Budget 2026-27, with most identifying infrastructure, real estate, pharma, and IT as the top sectoral plays for the upcoming fiscal year.

Sandeep Tandon, CIO of Quant Mutual Fund, called it a "conviction-building budget" that provides clarity for asset allocation. "We are overweight on three themes: domestic capex beneficiaries, consumption revival plays, and the healthcare ecosystem. The budget has reinforced our conviction in all three. We expect the Nifty to reach 27,000-28,000 by March 2027."

S Naren, ED and CIO of ICICI Prudential AMC, offered a more measured view. "The Budget is good but not transformative. Markets have already priced in most positives. We prefer a balanced approach — equal weight to value and growth, with a tilt towards large-caps given the elevated valuations in the mid and small-cap space."

Naren flagged the borrowing programme as a concern. "Rs 16.8 lakh crore of gross borrowing is not trivial. If the RBI doesn't step in with adequate OMOs, we could see crowding out of private credit. Bond investors should maintain a cautious stance on duration."

Nilesh Shah, managing director of Kotak Mahindra AMC, was bullish on the consumption theme. "The income tax cuts are a game-changer for discretionary consumption. Auto, retail, travel, and entertainment sectors will be direct beneficiaries. We are adding exposure to consumer discretionary stocks post-budget."

Shah also highlighted the real estate opportunity. "The housing stimulus is the most comprehensive we've seen. We are overweight on real estate developers, building materials, and home finance companies. The SWAMIH 2.0 fund will also revive stalled projects, which is positive for the entire value chain."

Preethi Krishna, fund manager at Aditya Birla Sun Life AMC, emphasized the defensive quality of the pharma allocation. "Healthcare is a multi-year structural story. The Budget's 150% R&D deduction, Ayushman Bharat expansion, and API self-sufficiency push create a favorable ecosystem. We're particularly positive on mid-cap pharma companies with strong R&D pipelines."

On the fixed income side, Rahul Goswami, CIO - fixed income at Franklin Templeton India, said the Budget had made the case for rate cuts stronger. "We are adding duration to our portfolios, with a preference for 10-15 year government securities. The fiscal consolidation path makes us confident that yields will trend lower. Our 12-month target for the 10-year yield is 6.30-6.40%."

The consensus among fund managers is that while the Budget is positive for markets, valuations warrant selectivity. "Buy quality, avoid froth" was the common refrain, with most managers advising investors to focus on companies with strong earnings visibility and reasonable valuations rather than chasing momentum.

"This Budget sets the stage for a strong earnings cycle. We expect Nifty EPS growth of 14-16% for FY27, driven by capex, consumption, and export recovery. At 22x forward earnings, the market is fairly valued — not cheap, but justifiable if earnings deliver," said Raamdeo Agrawal of Motilal Oswal.
author: Prashant Mahesh

title: Tax Expert Dissects Budget 2026: 'New Regime Now Clearly Superior, but Old Regime Loyalists Have Reasons to Stay'
content:
The Union Budget 2026-27 has made the new income tax regime significantly more attractive, but the old regime retains certain advantages for taxpayers with high deductions and investment commitments, according to Girish Vanvari, founder of Transaction Square and one of India's leading tax experts.

In an exclusive analysis for ET, Vanvari broke down the key personal tax changes and their implications:

The new regime now offers zero tax on income up to Rs 12 lakh (up from Rs 7 lakh), with the basic exemption limit raised to Rs 4 lakh and the standard deduction increased to Rs 1 lakh. The revised slabs are: 5% on Rs 4-8 lakh, 10% on Rs 8-12 lakh, 15% on Rs 12-16 lakh, 20% on Rs 16-20 lakh, 25% on Rs 20-25 lakh, and 30% above Rs 25 lakh.

"For someone earning Rs 15 lakh annually, the new regime now saves Rs 52,500 compared to the old regime without any deductions. Even with deductions of Rs 2.5 lakh under the old regime, the new regime comes out ahead by about Rs 15,000," Vanvari calculated.

However, he identified specific scenarios where the old regime remains beneficial: "If you have a home loan (Rs 3 lakh interest deduction now), significant Section 80C investments (Rs 1.5 lakh), NPS contributions (Rs 50,000 under 80CCD), and medical insurance (Rs 75,000 under 80D), the old regime can save you Rs 20,000-40,000 more at the Rs 20 lakh income level."

The Budget introduces a new Section 80CCD(1C) allowing additional NPS deduction of Rs 50,000 exclusively for employer contributions, available under both regimes. This is a significant sweetener for the new regime and is expected to boost NPS adoption among corporate employees.

Vanvari praised the simplification of TDS provisions. "The reduction of TDS rates on fixed deposits from 10% to 5% for non-senior citizens, and the raising of the threshold from Rs 40,000 to Rs 1 lakh, will significantly reduce compliance burden and improve cash flow for small savers."

The surcharge structure has been rationalized, with the peak surcharge on income above Rs 5 crore reduced from 37% to 25%, making the maximum effective tax rate 36.4% (down from 42.7%). "This is a welcome move that reduces the incentive for tax avoidance and makes India competitive with Singapore and Dubai for high-net-worth individuals," Vanvari noted.

Capital gains tax remains unchanged, with LTCG on equities at 12.5% (above Rs 1.25 lakh) and STCG at 20%. However, the Budget introduces indexation benefits for debt mutual funds held for more than three years, partially reversing the 2023 change that had eliminated this benefit. "This is a significant positive for debt fund investors and should boost flows back into debt mutual fund categories," Vanvari said.

The Budget also extends the deadline for filing updated returns from 2 years to 3 years, with a graduated additional tax structure — 25% for the first year, 50% for the second year, and 75% for the third year. "This gives more time for taxpayers to correct genuine errors, while the graduated penalty ensures voluntary compliance remains strong," Vanvari explained.
author: Lubna Kably

title: Budget 2026 vs Last 5 Budgets: How India's Fiscal Strategy Has Evolved from Pandemic Recovery to Growth Acceleration
content:
The Union Budget 2026-27 marks a decisive shift in India's fiscal narrative — from crisis management and recovery (FY21-FY23) to consolidation (FY24-FY26) and now growth acceleration (FY27). A comparative analysis of the last six budgets reveals how profoundly the government's fiscal strategy has evolved.

Fiscal Deficit Trajectory: The fiscal deficit has come down from a pandemic-high of 9.5% of GDP in FY21 to 4.4% in FY27 (budget estimate). The consolidation has been remarkably consistent — 6.7% in FY22, 6.4% in FY23, 5.6% in FY24, 5.1% in FY25, 4.8% in FY26 (RE), and now 4.4%. The government is on track to achieve sub-4% by FY28, as promised.

"The fiscal consolidation of 5.1 percentage points over six years, while maintaining a strong capex push, is among the most impressive fiscal adjustments globally," noted N.R. Bhanumurthy, vice-chancellor of BASE University and former NIPFP professor.

Capex Evolution: Perhaps the most dramatic transformation has been in capital expenditure. From Rs 3.36 lakh crore in FY21 (the pandemic year), capex has nearly quadrupled to Rs 12.5 lakh crore in FY27. As a share of GDP, capex has risen from 1.6% to 4.3%, creating a structural shift in the composition of government spending from revenue to capital.

The capex-to-revenue expenditure ratio has improved from 0.21 in FY21 to 0.31 in FY27, indicating that a greater share of every rupee spent is going towards asset creation. This has been achieved partly by rationalizing subsidies (food, fertilizer, and fuel subsidies have fallen from 3.9% of GDP in FY21 to 1.6% in FY27) and partly through buoyant revenue growth.

Tax Policy: The six-year period has seen a fundamental shift towards the new income tax regime. Budget 2020 introduced it with modest incentives; Budget 2023 made it the default; Budget 2025 enhanced it significantly; and Budget 2026 has made it the clearly superior option for most taxpayers. The effective income tax rate for someone earning Rs 15 lakh has fallen from 20.8% in FY21 to 13.1% in FY27.

Corporate tax collections have grown from Rs 6.6 lakh crore in FY21 to Rs 12.8 lakh crore (BE) in FY27, driven by the broadening of the tax base rather than rate increases. The corporate tax rate for new manufacturing companies remains at 15%, among the lowest in Asia.

Sector Allocation Shifts: Defence has seen the most consistent increases, from Rs 4.7 lakh crore in FY21 to Rs 6.8 lakh crore in FY27. Healthcare spending has doubled from 1.2% to 2.5% of GDP. Education allocation has risen from 2.8% to 3.5% of GDP.

The most notable shift is in green energy — from virtually zero specific allocation in FY21 to Rs 75,000 crore in FY27 through the new Green Energy Transition Fund, reflecting the government's commitment to the 2070 net-zero target.

"Looking at the six-year arc, what emerges is a clear strategic framework: stabilize (FY21-22), consolidate (FY23-25), accelerate (FY26-27). This Budget firmly puts India in the acceleration phase," said Sajjid Chinoy, chief India economist at JP Morgan.
author: Deepshikha Sikarwar

title: Income Tax Slab History: How Budget 2026 Gives the Biggest Relief Since 1997
content:
The income tax changes announced in the Union Budget 2026-27 represent the most significant relief to individual taxpayers since the landmark 1997 budget presented by P. Chidambaram, which slashed the peak tax rate from 40% to 30%. Budget 2026 doesn't change the peak rate but dramatically restructures slabs and exemptions to reduce the effective tax burden across income levels.

A historical analysis of India's income tax evolution reveals fascinating patterns:

The 1970s-80s Era: India's income tax rates peaked at an astonishing 97.75% (including surcharge) in 1973-74 under the Indira Gandhi government. The basic exemption limit was Rs 5,000. These confiscatory rates led to widespread tax evasion and a parallel economy estimated at 40-50% of GDP.

The 1985 Reforms: VP Singh's budget as Finance Minister began the rationalization, bringing the peak rate to 50% with three slabs. This was the first acknowledgment that lower rates could yield higher compliance.

The 1997 Big Bang: Chidambaram's 'Dream Budget' reduced the peak rate to 30% with a basic exemption of Rs 40,000. This budget is often credited with launching India's compliance-driven tax culture. Direct tax collections grew 20% in the following year.

The 2005-2014 Period: Gradual increases in exemption limits from Rs 50,000 to Rs 2.5 lakh, but the slab structure remained largely unchanged. The introduction of Section 80C with a Rs 1 lakh limit (later raised to Rs 1.5 lakh) became the primary savings incentive.

The 2020 New Regime: The introduction of a parallel lower-rate regime without deductions was the biggest structural change since 1997. Initial adoption was low (around 15% of taxpayers) due to unattractive rates.

Budget 2026 Transformation: The current budget makes the new regime decisively attractive with zero tax up to Rs 12 lakh. For context, in 1997, zero tax applied up to Rs 40,000. Adjusted for cumulative inflation of approximately 430% since then, the Rs 40,000 threshold would be equivalent to Rs 2.12 lakh today — meaning the Rs 12 lakh zero-tax threshold represents a real increase of nearly 6x in the tax-free income limit.

"In purchasing power terms, Budget 2026 is the most generous personal tax budget in independent India's history," said Parthasarathi Shome, former adviser to the Finance Minister and architect of several tax reforms.

The effective tax rates tell the story clearly. For a taxpayer earning Rs 12 lakh:
- FY2020 (old regime): Rs 1,17,000 (9.75%)
- FY2024 (new regime): Rs 60,000 (5%)
- FY2027 (new regime): Rs 0 (0%)

For Rs 20 lakh income:
- FY2020 (old regime): Rs 3,42,500 (17.1%)
- FY2024 (new regime): Rs 2,10,000 (10.5%)
- FY2027 (new regime): Rs 1,50,000 (7.5%)

The revenue cost of these cuts is estimated at Rs 1.1 lakh crore, which the government expects to partially recover through higher consumption-driven GST collections and improved compliance. The number of income tax return filers has grown from 6.9 crore in FY20 to 9.8 crore in FY26, and the government targets 12 crore filers by FY28.

"The direction is unmistakable — lower rates, fewer exemptions, broader base. India is converging towards the global best practice of simple, low-rate tax systems. Budget 2026 may be remembered as the tipping point," said Shome.
author: Lubna Kably

title: Capex Trajectory: From Rs 3.36 Lakh Crore to Rs 12.5 Lakh Crore — India's Infrastructure Spending Revolution Charted
content:
India's capital expenditure has undergone a tectonic shift over the past six fiscal years, rising from Rs 3.36 lakh crore in FY21 to Rs 12.5 lakh crore in FY27 — a compound annual growth rate of 24.5%. This infrastructure spending revolution, charted through six successive budgets, represents one of the most ambitious public investment programmes in the developing world.

The capex journey year by year:

FY21 (Rs 3.36 lakh crore, 1.6% of GDP): The pandemic year saw capex fall 10% from the budgeted Rs 4.12 lakh crore as resources were diverted to health and relief spending. Infrastructure projects stalled across the country.

FY22 (Rs 5.54 lakh crore, 2.3% of GDP): The recovery budget marked the beginning of the capex push, with a 65% jump over actuals. This was the inflection point, with the government signaling that investment, not consumption subsidies, would drive the recovery.

FY23 (Rs 7.28 lakh crore, 2.8% of GDP): A 31% increase consolidated the capex strategy. The PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan was launched, creating an integrated approach to infrastructure planning. Highway construction reached a record 12,162 km.

FY24 (Rs 9.50 lakh crore, 3.4% of GDP): The pre-election budget maintained the capex momentum with a 30% increase. Notably, the government exceeded its capex target for the first time, with actual spending at 98% of budget estimate. Vande Bharat train production crossed 50 trains.

FY25 (Rs 10.18 lakh crore, 3.5% of GDP): The post-election budget showed a more measured 7% increase, as the focus shifted to ensuring quality of spending. States were incentivized with Rs 1.5 lakh crore of interest-free capex loans.

FY26 (Rs 10.90 lakh crore RE, 3.7% of GDP): A modest 7% growth as fiscal consolidation took priority. However, utilization improved to 82% of BE, the highest in five years.

FY27 (Rs 12.5 lakh crore BE, 4.3% of GDP): Budget 2026 reignites the capex engine with a 14.7% jump. The effective capex (including state grants) reaches Rs 15.2 lakh crore or 5.2% of GDP.

The composition of capex has also evolved significantly. In FY21, railways and roads accounted for 58% of total capex. By FY27, their share has moderated to 39% as new categories — green energy (6%), urban infrastructure (11%), and digital infrastructure (5%) — have emerged as significant spending heads.

The economic impact is visible in hard data. India's logistics performance index ranking has improved from 44th in 2020 to 28th in 2025. National highway length has increased from 1.32 lakh km to 1.68 lakh km. Railway electrification has gone from 40% to 92% of the network.

"The capex push has fundamentally altered India's growth structure. We now have a genuine investment cycle driving growth, unlike the 2003-08 period which was largely credit-fuelled. This makes the current growth phase more sustainable," said Pranjul Bhandari, chief India economist at HSBC.

However, concerns remain about absorption capacity. The CAG has flagged quality issues in highway construction, and several metro projects face cost and time overruns. "Spending more is necessary but not sufficient. We need to spend better. The government should invest in project management capacity as aggressively as it invests in physical infrastructure," cautioned former Finance Secretary Subhash Chandra Garg.

Looking ahead, the government's National Infrastructure Pipeline targets cumulative investment of Rs 111 lakh crore by FY30, of which public sector (central and state) is expected to contribute 40%. If the current trajectory holds, this target appears achievable.
author: Vinay Pandey

title: Budget 2026 New Tax Slabs Explained: Zero Tax Up to Rs 12 Lakh, Middle Class Saves Up to Rs 1.1 Lakh
content:
Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman announced the most sweeping overhaul of personal income tax slabs in the Union Budget 2026-27, making income up to Rs 12 lakh effectively tax-free under the new regime and delivering savings of up to Rs 1,10,000 for middle-class taxpayers earning Rs 25 lakh per annum.

The revised tax slabs under the new regime for FY27 are:
- Up to Rs 4 lakh: Nil
- Rs 4 lakh to Rs 8 lakh: 5%
- Rs 8 lakh to Rs 12 lakh: 10%
- Rs 12 lakh to Rs 16 lakh: 15%
- Rs 16 lakh to Rs 20 lakh: 20%
- Rs 20 lakh to Rs 25 lakh: 25%
- Above Rs 25 lakh: 30%

A special rebate under Section 87A ensures that individuals with taxable income up to Rs 12 lakh pay zero tax. The rebate amount has been increased to Rs 80,000 from Rs 25,000, fully offsetting the tax liability at Rs 12 lakh income.

The standard deduction has been enhanced from Rs 75,000 to Rs 1 lakh under the new regime, meaning salaried employees earning up to Rs 13 lakh (gross) will effectively pay no income tax.

Here's how the savings work out at various income levels:

- Rs 10 lakh income: Tax falls from Rs 42,500 (FY26) to Rs 0 (FY27). Saving: Rs 42,500
- Rs 12 lakh income: Tax falls from Rs 72,500 to Rs 0. Saving: Rs 72,500
- Rs 15 lakh income: Tax falls from Rs 1,27,500 to Rs 75,000. Saving: Rs 52,500
- Rs 20 lakh income: Tax falls from Rs 2,60,000 to Rs 1,75,000. Saving: Rs 85,000
- Rs 25 lakh income: Tax falls from Rs 3,85,000 to Rs 2,75,000. Saving: Rs 1,10,000
- Rs 50 lakh income: Tax falls from Rs 11,10,000 to Rs 10,25,000. Saving: Rs 85,000

"The government has achieved the trifecta — simplified slabs, lower rates, and a higher zero-tax threshold. An individual earning Rs 1 lakh per month paying zero tax was unthinkable even two years ago," said Kuldip Kumar, former partner at PricewaterhouseCoopers India.

The revenue forgone is estimated at Rs 1,10,000 crore. The government expects to recover approximately Rs 40,000-50,000 crore through increased GST collections (as higher disposable income drives spending) and improved tax compliance.

The new slabs are expected to accelerate the migration from the old to new tax regime. Currently, 56% of taxpayers are on the new regime. The government projects this will rise to 80% by FY28. However, a significant minority — particularly those with large home loan deductions and disciplined 80C investors — may still find the old regime beneficial.

The Budget also raises the threshold for tax audit under Section 44AB from Rs 1 crore to Rs 1.5 crore for businesses and from Rs 50 lakh to Rs 75 lakh for professionals, easing the compliance burden on small businesses.

"This Budget will fundamentally change the relationship between the average taxpayer and the government. When people see their tax liability drop to zero at Rs 12 lakh, it builds trust and encourages voluntary compliance," said Revenue Secretary Sanjay Malhotra at the post-Budget press conference.
author: ET Bureau

title: Budget 2026 HRA Changes: Metro Exemption Raised to 60%, Two New Cities Added; What It Means for You
content:
The Union Budget 2026-27 has revamped the House Rent Allowance (HRA) exemption structure, raising the metro city limit from 50% to 60% of basic salary and adding Pune and Ahmedabad to the list of metro cities. For non-metro cities, the exemption has been raised from 40% to 50%. These changes apply under both the old and new tax regimes.

The revised HRA exemption rules for FY27:

Metro cities (Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata, Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Pune, Ahmedabad): The exemption is the lowest of — (a) actual HRA received, (b) 60% of basic salary (up from 50%), or (c) rent paid minus 10% of basic salary.

Non-metro cities: The exemption is the lowest of — (a) actual HRA received, (b) 50% of basic salary (up from 40%), or (c) rent paid minus 10% of basic salary.

Let us illustrate the impact with an example. Consider a salaried employee in Mumbai with a basic salary of Rs 80,000 per month, HRA of Rs 40,000, and rent of Rs 35,000:

Under old rules: Exemption = lowest of Rs 40,000, Rs 40,000 (50% of basic), or Rs 27,000 (rent minus 10% of basic) = Rs 27,000/month = Rs 3,24,000/year.

Under new rules: Exemption = lowest of Rs 40,000, Rs 48,000 (60% of basic), or Rs 27,000 = Rs 27,000/month = Rs 3,24,000/year. No change in this case because the rent-minus-10% formula is the binding constraint.

But for a higher salary employee in Mumbai with basic of Rs 1,50,000, HRA of Rs 75,000, and rent of Rs 80,000:

Under old rules: Exemption = lowest of Rs 75,000, Rs 75,000 (50% of basic), or Rs 65,000 = Rs 65,000/month.

Under new rules: Exemption = lowest of Rs 75,000, Rs 90,000 (60% of basic), or Rs 65,000 = Rs 65,000/month. Again, no change.

"The HRA change is most impactful for employees in the mid-salary range (Rs 40,000-80,000 basic) where the 50%/60% formula is typically the binding constraint rather than actual rent paid," explained Archit Gupta, CEO of Clear (formerly ClearTax).

The addition of Pune and Ahmedabad to the metro city list is significant. An employee in Pune with a basic salary of Rs 60,000 who was getting 40% exemption (Rs 24,000) will now get 60% (Rs 36,000) — a difference of Rs 1,44,000 per year. At the 30% tax rate, this translates to an annual tax saving of Rs 43,200.

"Pune and Ahmedabad have long deserved metro status for HRA purposes. Rental costs in these cities are comparable to Chennai and Hyderabad. This correction was overdue," said Sudhir Kaushik, co-founder and CEO of TaxSpanner.

The Budget also clarifies that for the new tax regime, HRA exemption is available only for salaried employees receiving HRA as part of their salary structure. Self-employed individuals and those not receiving HRA cannot claim this benefit under either regime.

Importantly, the government has increased the deduction limit for rent paid by those not receiving HRA (under Section 80GG, applicable only in the old regime) from Rs 5,000 to Rs 10,000 per month, benefiting self-employed individuals and informal sector workers.
author: Preeti Kulkarni

title: NPS Gets Major Budget Boost: Employer Contribution Limit Raised to 14%, New Partial Withdrawal Rules
content:
The National Pension System (NPS) has received its most significant enhancement since inception in the Union Budget 2026-27, with the employer contribution deduction limit being raised from 10% to 14% of basic salary for private sector employees (matching the government employee benefit) and the introduction of more flexible partial withdrawal rules.

The key NPS changes in Budget 2026:

1. Employer Contribution: The deduction limit under Section 80CCD(2) has been raised from 10% to 14% of basic salary for private sector employees. For government employees, this was already at 14%. The change creates parity and is expected to significantly boost NPS adoption in the corporate sector.

For an employee with a basic salary of Rs 1 lakh per month, this means the employer can now contribute Rs 14,000 (up from Rs 10,000) to NPS with full tax deductibility. The additional Rs 4,000 monthly contribution, growing at 10% annually over a 25-year career, could add Rs 1.2-1.5 crore to the retirement corpus.

2. New Section 80CCD(1C): A new section allows an additional deduction of Rs 50,000 for employer NPS contributions, available under both old and new tax regimes. This is over and above the 14% limit and is designed to incentivize companies to offer NPS as a core benefit.

3. Partial Withdrawal: The rules for partial withdrawal have been liberalized significantly. Previously, subscribers could withdraw up to 25% of their own contributions after 3 years for specified purposes (education, medical treatment, home purchase). Now, the limit has been raised to 40% and the lock-in period reduced to 2 years. The number of permitted withdrawals has been increased from 3 to 5 during the subscription period.

4. Annuity Flexibility: The mandatory annuity purchase at retirement has been reduced from 40% to 25% of the corpus. This means subscribers can now withdraw 75% as a tax-free lump sum (up from 60%), significantly improving the liquidity and attractiveness of NPS.

"This is a landmark budget for NPS. The 14% employer contribution parity, combined with the reduced annuity requirement, addresses the two biggest objections that private sector employees had about NPS — lower employer contribution and forced annuity purchase," said Sumit Shukla, CEO of HDFC Pension.

The Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority (PFRDA) has set an ambitious target of 10 crore NPS subscribers by 2028, up from the current 7.2 crore. The Budget changes are expected to add 50-60 lakh new corporate subscribers over the next two years.

Financial planners are advising their clients to reconsider NPS allocation in light of the changes. "With the effective tax benefit of up to Rs 2.74 lakh per year (14% employer contribution + Rs 50,000 80CCD(2) + Rs 50,000 80CCD(1B)), NPS is now the single most tax-efficient retirement savings instrument in India," said Vishal Dhawan, founder of Plan Ahead Wealth Advisors.

The Budget also introduces a 'NPS Vatsalya Plus' scheme specifically for parents contributing to their children's NPS accounts, with a matching contribution of up to Rs 1,000 per year from the government for accounts belonging to families below the income tax threshold. This is expected to promote the savings habit and financial inclusion from an early age.

However, some experts cautioned that the changes may not be sufficient to compete with EPF for lower-income employees. "For someone earning below Rs 15,000 per month, EPF's guaranteed 8.25% return still looks more attractive than NPS's market-linked returns. The government needs to consider offering a minimum return guarantee for lower-income NPS subscribers," said D. Bhalla, former secretary of the Labour Ministry.
author: Babar Zaidi

title: Standard Deduction Hiked to Rs 1 Lakh: Budget 2026 Puts Rs 8,300 More Per Month in Salaried Hands
content:
The Union Budget 2026-27 has raised the standard deduction for salaried employees from Rs 75,000 to Rs 1 lakh — a Rs 25,000 increase that effectively puts Rs 625-2,083 per month back into the pockets of taxpayers depending on their tax bracket. Pensioners also benefit, with the standard deduction on pension income raised from Rs 50,000 to Rs 75,000.

The standard deduction, reintroduced in Budget 2018 at Rs 40,000 (after being scrapped in 2005), has now been raised four times:
- Budget 2018: Rs 40,000 (replacing transport allowance and medical reimbursement)
- Budget 2019: Rs 50,000
- Budget 2024: Rs 75,000 (under new regime)
- Budget 2026: Rs 1,00,000 (under both regimes)

The key significance of the Budget 2026 change is that the Rs 1 lakh standard deduction is now available under both the old and new tax regimes. Previously, the enhanced Rs 75,000 deduction was available only under the new regime, creating an asymmetry that caused confusion among taxpayers.

The tax savings from the enhanced standard deduction by income level:
- Rs 8-12 lakh bracket (10% slab): Rs 2,500 annual saving (Rs 208/month)
- Rs 12-16 lakh bracket (15% slab): Rs 3,750 annual saving (Rs 313/month)
- Rs 16-20 lakh bracket (20% slab): Rs 5,000 annual saving (Rs 417/month)
- Rs 20-25 lakh bracket (25% slab): Rs 6,250 annual saving (Rs 521/month)
- Above Rs 25 lakh (30% slab): Rs 7,500 annual saving (Rs 625/month)
- Above Rs 25 lakh (30% + surcharge): Up to Rs 10,000+ annual saving

When combined with the tax slab changes, the total relief for a salaried employee earning Rs 15 lakh annually comes to approximately Rs 55,000-60,000 per year, or roughly Rs 4,700-5,000 per month. For someone at Rs 25 lakh, the combined benefit is Rs 1,10,000-1,17,000 per year, or about Rs 9,200-9,750 per month.

"The standard deduction increase is a clean, simple measure that benefits every salaried person without requiring any investment or documentation. It's the most efficient way to deliver tax relief," said Naveen Wadhwa, deputy general manager at Taxmann.

The government has also clarified that the standard deduction applies to income from salary and pension only — freelancers and self-employed individuals cannot claim it. However, the Budget introduces a new 'Professional Standard Deduction' of Rs 50,000 for professionals with gross receipts up to Rs 75 lakh who opt for the presumptive taxation scheme under Section 44ADA.

The cumulative impact of the standard deduction hike and tax slab changes is expected to boost urban consumption significantly. "At an aggregate level, the income tax relief puts Rs 1.1 lakh crore back into consumers' hands. Even if only 60-65% of this is spent, it translates to Rs 65,000-70,000 crore of additional consumption demand," noted Abheek Barua, chief economist at HDFC Bank.

Retailers and consumer companies are already anticipating the demand boost. "We expect the Budget to drive a 10-12% increase in discretionary spending in metros. Electronics, apparel, and dining out should be the primary beneficiaries," said Kumar Rajagopalan, CEO of the Retailers Association of India.

The Finance Ministry has also announced plans to simplify the salary income computation, proposing to merge all standard allowances (including the proposed professional tax deduction under Section 16) into the standard deduction in future budgets. "The long-term vision is to have a single deduction figure that replaces all allowances and deductions from salary income, making it truly simple for the salaried class," said a senior tax official.
author: ET Bureau
